coredata.dc:title: A solid-phase luminescence sensor based on molecularly imprinted polymer-CdSeS/ZnS quantum dots for selective extraction and detection of sulfasalazine in biological samples
coredata.dc:creator: Ahmadpour, Hamidreza
Hosseini, Seyed Mohamadreza Milani
coredata.dc:description: A new solid phase luminescence sensor (SPLS) based on molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) as recognition element and semi-conductor CdSeS/ZnS alloyed quantum dots (QDs) as fluorophore has been synthesized and developed for the determination of sulfasalazine. The surface modified quantum dot was attached to the surface of the glass slide. MIP and Non-imprinted polymer (NIP) with the recognition site for Sulfasalazine (SSZ) were synthesized and attached to the immobilized QDs. A trace amount of sulfasalazine was determined using fluorescence spectroscopy method based on quenching effect of sulfasalazine on the QDs luminescence with a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. The MIP-QDs based sensor showed high recognition capability of sulfasalazine in comparison with the NIP-QDs based sensor. Various experimental parameters affecting fluorescence intensity such as the reactions time and pH were investigated and optimized. Under optimal conditions, the MIP-QDs sensor showed good linearity for sulfasalazine in the range of 0.02–1.5 µmol L−1 with a determination coefficient (R2) of 0.9986. The limit of detection (S/N = 3) and limit of quantification (S/N = 10) were found to be 0.0071 and 0.024 µmol L−1, respectively. The intra-day and inter-day RSDs were 2.6–5.1% and 3.5–6.9%, respectively. The MIP-QDs based sensor was successfully used to the determination of sulfasalazine in human plasma and urine with the recoveries in the range of 90–107%, offering reliability and applicability of the sensor to the complex matrices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfasalazine
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Quantum dot
Fluorescence detection
Solid phase extraction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the amino-functionalized glass slide
Synthesis of the QDs bonded amino-functionalized glass slide
Real sample collection, storage, and preparation
Reusability and reproducibility of MIP-QDs glass slide
Analytical characteristics and real samples analysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Research Council of The Iran University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The Support by the Research Council of The Iran University of Science and Technology through Grant to conduct this study is gratefully acknowledged. The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis procedure of fluorescence MIP-QDs-glass slide sensor.
SEM images of a) amino-functionalized glass slide; b) QDs-glass slide; and c) MIP-QDs-glass slide.

a) 3D-excitation-emission fluorescence spectra of the bare glass slide; b) 3D-excitation-emission fluorescence spectra of the MIP-QDs-functionalized glass slide; and c) fluorescence spectra of the glass slide, QDs-glass slide, and MIP-QDs-glass slide.
EDX analysis of the MIP-QDs-glass slide.

a) UV–Vis– absorption spectra of SSZ and fluorescence emission spectra of the CdSeS/ZnS alloyed QDs; b) Effect of the sample solution pH on the fluorescence quenching of SSZ; c) Effect of the reaction time on the fluorescence quenching of SSZ.
Quenching efficiency of the MIP-QDs-glass slide and NIP-QDs-glass slide for SSZ and its structural analogs.
The fluorescence spectra and the Stern-Volmer plot of the MIP-QDs sensor at different concentrations of SSZ.
Obtained results from determination of SSZ in plasma samples.
Tolerance limit of some interfering ions and substances in the determination of SSZ using MIP-QDs based sensor.
Comparison of the developed method with other reported methods for determination of SSZ.

row.entry: Tap water, milk, Honey and human Plasma

head.ce:title: A solid-phase luminescence sensor based on molecularly imprinted polymer-CdSeS/ZnS quantum dots for selective extraction and detection of sulfasalazine in biological samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Real Samples Analysis, Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Iran University of Science and Technology, P.O. Box 1684613114, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Real Samples Analysis, Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Iran University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new solid phase luminescence sensor (SPLS) based on molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) as recognition element and semi-conductor CdSeS/ZnS alloyed quantum dots (QDs) as fluorophore has been synthesized and developed for the determination of sulfasalazine. The surface modified quantum dot was attached to the surface of the glass slide. MIP and Non-imprinted polymer (NIP) with the recognition site for Sulfasalazine (SSZ) were synthesized and attached to the immobilized QDs. A trace amount of sulfasalazine was determined using fluorescence spectroscopy method based on quenching effect of sulfasalazine on the QDs luminescence with a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. The MIP-QDs based sensor showed high recognition capability of sulfasalazine in comparison with the NIP-QDs based sensor. Various experimental parameters affecting fluorescence intensity such as the reactions time and pH were investigated and optimized. Under optimal conditions, the MIP-QDs sensor showed good linearity for sulfasalazine in the range of 0.02–1.5 µmol L−1 with a determination coefficient (R2) of 0.9986. The limit of detection (S/N = 3) and limit of quantification (S/N = 10) were found to be 0.0071 and 0.024 µmol L−1, respectively. The intra-day and inter-day RSDs were 2.6–5.1% and 3.5–6.9%, respectively. The MIP-QDs based sensor was successfully used to the determination of sulfasalazine in human plasma and urine with the recoveries in the range of 90–107%, offering reliability and applicability of the sensor to the complex matrices.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfasalazine (SSZ) as a synthetic antibiotic was designed in 1938 for the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis and is the product of the reaction between an antibacterial agent (sulfapyridine) and an anti-inflammatory agent (salicylic acid) [1]. SSZ has immunosuppressive effects [2] and controls active ulcerative colitis or Crohn's disease [3]. However, its side reactions can cause a disorder of the hemopoietic system or make carcinogenic effects [4]. In addition, secondary dose-dependent side effects were observed in 3–20% of treated patients [5,6], so controlling the level of SSZ in the patient serum may help to prevent the drawbacks of this medicine. The SSZ concentration in the patient serum is reported in the range of 6–32 µg/mL. Thus, development of a fast and sensitive method to the SSZ determination would be grateful for such therapeutic application [4].

So far different instrumental tools for determination of SSZ and its metabolites including LC [7,8], HPLC [9–12], near-infrared spectroscopy (NIR) [13], chemiluminescence [14], Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [15], capillary electrophoresis–mass spectrometry (CE-MS) [16], electrochemical detection [4] and resonance light scattering (RLS) [17] have been reported. Although these methods are robust and accurate, some of them need a separate sample cleanup step and require expensive instrumentations to achieve lower detection limit.

One of the most promising techniques for lower detection limit achievement is fluorescence spectroscopy with extremely high sensitivity, large linear dynamic range and low detection limit down to ng L-1 level [18]. Unfortunately, a limited number of analytes are fluorophore so fluorimetry would not always be a possible way. Using another fluorophore as an intermediate analytical probe and main analyte as its quencher is an approach to use the advantages of fluorescence spectroscopy for analyzing non-fluorescence analytes. This quenching occurs via energy transfer between excited fluorescence agent (donor) and analyte molecule (acceptor) [19]. Quantum dots (QDs) which has been used as fluorophores in fluorescent sensors, probes, and tags in various applications are basically semiconductor nanoparticles that holding unique characters such as size tunability, narrow emission spectra, high luminescence quantum yield, photostability, and well biocompatibility [20]. QDs are greatly useful analytical assays because show a Stern-Volmer quenching behavior [21] where decreasing in fluorescence intensity of QDs can be mathematically related to the concentration of quencher [19].

Although functionalization of QDs with different functional groups provide a simple approach to analyze different compounds [22–27], they suffer lack of appropriate selectivity. Recently using molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with recognition towards target molecules (template) known as a new way to improve the selectivity of quantum dots [28–33]. MIPs are artificial receptors that is synthesized starting with a mixture of polymerizable monomer and template molecule which have reversible interactions like electrostatic, hydrogen bonding and van der Waals. The polymerization of this mixture is initiated with heat or UV light with the presence of a cross-linker. Polymerization process fixes the relative position of the functional monomers around the template molecule in a 3D network. The template molecule then removed with an extraction solvent and leave cavities with complementary size, shape and functional group distribution to those of the template [34]. This technique can be used for extraction and pre-concentration of analytes in the liquid samples [35].

In this work, a new solid phase luminescence sensor (SPLS) composed of MIP with recognition sites for SSZ conjugated with immobilized CdSeS/ZnS alloyed QDs on the surface of a glass slide was successfully fabricated and the final analytical characteristics of this sensor were examined. The relationship between fluorescence quenching of the fabricated SPLS and the concentration of SSZ was also investigated. Determination of analyte with the developed luminescent sensor can eliminate some problems associated with other adsorbents in the solution like MIP-QDs nanoparticles such as difficulty to recycle the adsorbent from the sample solution and reusability. Since in here the MIP-QDs nanoparticles are fixed on the surface of the glass slide, it can simply be taken out from the sample solution by just a forceps without the need to additional centrifugation, filtration or magnetic separation. In addition, the SSZ preconcentration by a solvent free solid phase extraction and subsequent fluorescence determination enhances the sensitivity of the method. This fabricated sensor was used as a simple, rapid, and selective detection tool for SSZ determination in biological samples.

All reagents used were of analytical grade. The deionized water was used throughout the experiment. Microscope glass slides (76 mm × 26 mm ×1 mm) were purchased from ISOLAB (Wertheim, Germany). CdSeS/ZnS alloyed quantum dots, Sulfasalazine (SSZ) and 2-Azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

Ammonia (25% aqueous solution), hydrogen peroxide (30 wt% solution in H2O), hydrochloric acid (37% in water), methanol (99.9%), toluene (99.0%), DMF (99.8%), Acetonitrile (99.9%), Acetic acid, Dichloromethane (DCM), N,N-Diisopropylethylamine (DIPEA), (3-Aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), 2-(1H-Benzotriazole-1-yl)-1,1,3,3-tetramethyluronium hexafluorophosphate (HBTU), 3-Mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), 3-(mercaptopropyl)trimethoxysilane (MPS), Methacryloxypropyl trimethoxysilane (Silane A), Methacrylic acid (MAA) and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany).

The luminescence spectrums were acquired using Spectrofluorophotometer RF-6000 equipped with Solid Sample Holder (Shimadzu, Japan). The surface morphology and elemental analyses were investigated using a scanning electron microscope (Mira 3 TESCAN, Czech Republic) equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS, SAMx, France). A Universal 320R refrigerated centrifuge equipped with an angle rotor (6-place, 9000 rpm, Cat. No.1620A) was used for synthesis procedure. Corning PH meter (Model 140) with a combined glass electrode was used for pH measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the amino-functionalized glass slide
ce:section.ce:para: Amino- functionalization of glass surface was done on the basis of the previously reported studies [36,37] with some modifications. Firstly, microscope glass slide was prewashed with (NH3, H2O2, and deionized water: 15,15,70% V/V) and then with (HCl, H2O2, and deionized water: 15,15,70% V/V). After that, the glass slide was rinsed with deionized water, dried and transferred to a reflux balloon containing 2.5% of APTES in anhydrous toluene and incubated for 12 h at 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the QDs bonded amino-functionalized glass slide
ce:section.ce:para: To surface modification of amino-functionalized glass slide with QDs, at first, the purchased QDs were reacted with MPA as described elsewhere [38]. Briefly, 1 mL of purchased QDs (1000 ppm), 100 µl MPA, 1 mL Hunig base and 2 mL Chloroform were refluxed under an argon stream for 2 h. The precipitate was then washed with chloroform three times, dried and dispersed in DMF. In the next step the amino-functionalized glass slide was placed in a staining jar containing dispersed QDs in DMF, 1 mL of HTBU (1000 ppm in DMF) and 4 µl Hunig base and stirred for 24 h. Finally, the glass slides with immobilized QDs were rinsed with DMF, DMF/methanol, and methanol.

To cover and protect the surface of QDs with a silicon oxide layer, the prepared QDs-glass slide in the previous step was placed into a glass staining jar containing 90 mL of anhydrous methanol, 1 mL of the Hunig base and 200 µl of MPS. This mixture was purged with argon and incubated for 1 h at 60 °C, cooled to the room temperature and stored for 1 day In order to functionalization of QDs surfaces with methacryloxypropyl trimethoxysilane (Silane A), the slide was placed in a mixture of 200 µl of Silane A, 1 mL of the Hunig base and 100 mL of anhydrous toluene and left at ambient temperature for 1 day. Finally, the slide was thoroughy washed with toluene, DCM, and acetonitrile.

The MIP and NIP modified QDs-glass slide was prepared as follow. To prepare MIP-QDs glass slide, the synthesized QDs-glass slide in the previous step was immersed in a mixture containing 85 mL of acetonitrile and 25 mL of toluene, 0.2 mmol of Sulfasalazine (SSZ), 6 mmol of Methacrylic acid (MAA), 15 mmol of ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) and 0.3 mmol of 2-Azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN). The polymerization reaction was started by heating the mixture to 60 °C for 2 h and keeping for 1 day. The glass slide was then washed with a mixture of methanol/acetic acid (8:2) to the removal of unreacted monomers and SSZ template molecules from the MIP. The non-imprinted polymer (NIP) was prepared with the similar procedure of MIP, with the exception that no SSZ was used as the template molecule. Finally, the glass slides were dried under vacuum at 40 °C for 1 day. Fig. 1 shows a schematic illustration of the synthesis steps for MIP-QDs-glass slide.

To test the efficiency of the new fabricated SPLS for determination of SSZ, a solvent-less solid phase extraction procedure was developed. Firstly, the prepared glass slide was immersed in 20 mL of sample solution at pH 4.5 containing Britton–Robinson buffer and then slowly shaken for 10 min. After that, the slide was washed with deionized water and dried under a stream of argon subsequently. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of the dry slide was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample collection, storage, and preparation
ce:section.ce:para: Plasma and urine samples used as the blank sample matrices were collected from volunteers. The samples were collected into sterilized containers and stored at −20 °C prior to the analysis. Prior to the extraction step, the samples were located at room temperature to defrost and then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 3 min to remove any precipitation, if present. The plasma sample was diluted in the ratio 1:2 (v:v) using deionized water before analyzing.

The morphology of the amino-functionalized glass slide, QDs functionalized glass slide and QDs functionalized glass slide modified with MIP was investigated by SEM. The SEM image of the amino-functionalized glass slide ( Fig. 2a) showed a relatively homogeneous and smoother surface than the surface of QDs-glass slide (Fig. 2b) and MIP-QDs-glass slide (Fig. 2c); indicating this morphology is very much different from that of the surface resulted from the embedment of the polymer or QDs. The contrast between the surface morphology of the pre-MIP and post-MIP modified QDs-glass slide was also very distinct in SEM images (Fig. 2b and Fig. 2c). These results confirm the successful functionalization of the glass slide with QDs and MIP.

The fluorescence property of bare glass slide, QDs-glass slide, and MIP-QDs-glass slide was also examined. Three-dimensional excitation-emission fluorescence spectra of the bare glass slide and MIP-QDs-functionalized glass slide were displayed in Fig. 3a and Fig. 3b, respectively. The detailed patterns produced by 3D fluorescence spectroscopy clearly confirm the successful functionalization of the glass slide with MIP-QDs. Fig. 3c shows the fluorescence spectra of the glass slide, QDs-glass slide, and MIP-QDs-glass slide. As can be seen, no fluorescence intensity was observed for blank glass slide at excitation wavelength while the fluorescence intensity of MIP-QDs-glass slide showed a partly decreased in comparison with that of the QDs-glass slide. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of MIP-QDs-glass slide was sufficiently large to cause fluorescence quenching by SSZ.

The fluorescence stability was also evaluated by the repeated measuring of the fluorescence intensity every 5 min for one hour and the same measuring (five replicate) every day for one week. The results showed a stable emission of the MIP-QDs-glass slide (> 95% of the initial intensity) within 60 min and one week. This indicated that the developed fluorescence sensor has acceptable stability. All of the results of fluorescence spectra confirmed the successful synthesis of MIP functionalized QDs-glass slide.

Elemental analysis was investigated by energy dispersive x-ray (EDX) to obtain the chemical composition of the synthesized MIP-QDs-glass slide as shown in Fig. 4. The results indicate the presence of C, O, Na, Mg, Al, Si, Ca, S, Zn, Se and Cd in the structure which clearly confirm the existence of CdSeS/ZnS quantum dots and successful synthesis of MIP-QDs-glass slide.

This study used MIP-QDs bonded glass slide as a solid phase luminescence sensor to detect sulfasalazine based on the fluorescence quenching of SSZ bonded to the MIP-QDs. As shown in Fig. 5a, the absorption wavelength for the SSZ is about 320–460 nm and the emission band gap for the QDs is located about 400–500 nm which indicating a suitable overlap for the two spectra. Therefore, the possible mechanism of the decrease in fluorescence intensity can involve fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [39–41]. In this mechanism, the energy of the QDs was transferred to the SSZ molecules that are bonded to the polymer, which would result in the fluorescence quenching of the QDs.

When SSZ molecules bonded to the MIP located on QDs slide, an apparent fluorescence quenching was observed. To achieve the best sensitivity and obtain optimal SPE conditions, several parameters of the system including the pH of the sample solution and reaction time were investigated and optimized. Since the extraction procedure is a solvent-free solid phase extraction, there is no need to optimize the other experimental conditions in the traditional solid phase based extraction methods such as type and volume of the extraction solvent (eluent) or the required time to desorb the analytes from the adsorbent. This makes the developed procedure faster, greener and more convenient than other SPE based methods.

The fluorescence quenching in this system followed the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (1)):
(1)
F
0
/
F
=
1 +
K
SV
C
q
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the MIP-QDs in the absence and presence of quencher, respectively, KSV is the quenching constant of the quencher and Cq is the quencher concentration. The ratio of (F0-F)/F0 was used to calculate the fluorescence quenching efficiency.

The pH of the solution can influence the enhancement or reduction of MIP-QDs fluorescence intensity. As demonstrated in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence quenching of the sensor was increased with increasing pH from 1.0 to 4.5 and then decreased at pH in the range of 4.5–9.0. In the pH range of 3.0–4.5, SSZ molecules can attach to the binding sites of MIP via hydrogen bonding interactions. On the other hand, the free carboxylic groups on the surface of the polymer are ionized at pH values higher than pKa of polymer (pKa = 4.7), leading to a reduction in MIP interaction with SSZ. The results revealed that the fluorescence response of the detection system strongly depends on the pH values of the medium. Therefore, in all other experiments, the pH of the sample solution was set at 4.5 by a buffer.

The binding of SSZ to MIP-QDs surface is an equilibrium reaction and thus, there is a direct relationship between the bonded amount of analyte into the QDs-MIP glass slide and the reaction time which can affect the fluorescence quenching process. In this study, the contact time between MIP-QDs slide and sample solution was investigated from 1 to 20 min. As shown in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence quenching of MIP-QDs was increased with increasing the reaction time up to 10 min and no change in the fluorescence intensity was observed for the longer times, probably due to saturation of the binding sites at the surface of the MIP-QDs sensor.

The selectivity of the MIP-bonded QDs and NIP-QDs were studied using SSZ as template molecule and three structural analogs of the SSZ including sulfadiazine, sulfamethoxazole, and sulfapyridine as competitive compounds. Fluorescence quenching depends on the absorptive affinity of the molecules with the template. It was obviously seen that the fluorescence intensity reduction of the MIP-QDs was much larger than that of the NIP-QDs by the SSZ as template molecule at the same concentration ( Fig. 6). In addition, the Fluorescence quenching of the MIP-QDs for SSZ was much higher than that other competitive compounds while no fluorescence quenching was observed for all substances when using NIP-QDs (Fig. 6). These results revealed that the recognition mechanism of the MIP-QDs is based on interactions of the size, shape, and functionality of the template. For the NIP, nonspecific binding was the primary factor between them, therefore no significant change in fluorescence intensity was observed. Thus the synthesized MIP-QDs bonded glass slide can be applied for high selectivity recognition of SSZ without the interference of other competitive compounds

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability and reproducibility of MIP-QDs glass slide
ce:section.ce:para: Regeneration and reuse of adsorbent in SPE-base procedures are a very important aspect in term of economy and environmental protection. The ability to reuse of adsorbent can reduce the need for new adsorbent production and the problem of waste of used adsorbent. In order to investigate the reusability of the prepared MIP-QDs-glass slides, after completing the analysis, the slide was carefully washed with methanol to remove SSZ molecules from MIP templates. The slide was then washed with distillated water and reused for the next procedure according to Section 2.4 (data not shown). The results displayed that the MIP-QDs slides can be reused at least 10 times without any significant effect on the fluorescence intensity indicating the potential of the sensor for long-term uses in the detection of SSZ which make the treatment method more economical and environmentally friendly.

Repeatability of the results for different synthesized MIP-QDs-glass slides is another important factor that can be investigated. In order to evaluate the reproducibility of the sensor, five MIP-QDs- glass slides were synthesized (according to Section 2.3) and then applied to SSZ determination (at 0.2 µmol L-1) according to the procedure. Five replicated analysis were performed for each slide and the mean fluorescence intensities and their variances were calculated. The results were compared based on the variances and difference between mean fluorescence intensities with Tukey-test at p < 0.05. Based on the results, the RSDs were obtained between 2.1% and 4.3% and no significant difference was observed in the mean fluorescence intensities indicating the good reproducibility of the prepared sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical characteristics and real samples analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performances of the sensor were investigated under optimum conditions mentioned above. Calibration curves were obtained by plotting fluorescence intensities ratio (F0/F) versus concentrations of SSZ. The limits of detection (LODs) and quantification (LOQs) of the method based on a signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio of 3 and 10 were obtained 0.0071 and 0.024 µmol L-1, respectively. Good linearity was observed over the concentration range of 0.02–1.5 µmol L-1 with a satisfactory determination coefficient (R2) of 0.9986. The fluorescence spectra and the Stern-Volmer plot of MIP-QDs with different concentrations of SSZ were shown in Fig. 7. The Fluorescence quenching of MIP-QDs with SSZ was satisfied with the following equation: F0/F = 3.7166CSSZ + 1.2335.

The precision of the method was studied at the concentration level of 0.20 µmol L-1 SSZ. The intra- and inter-day precisions were obtained 2.9% and 5.3% by performing the SSZ detection procedure five times in the same day (n = 5), and five replicate in three consecutive days (n = 15), respectively.

In order to evaluate the practicality of the proposed method based on MIP-QDs sensor, a recovery assay was used to detect SSZ in biological samples (plasma and urine). To determine the recoveries, the biological samples were spiked with SSZ at two concentration levels of 0.2 and 1.0 µmol L-1, respectively. The results are shown in Table 1. As can be seen, all recoveries were in the range of 90–107% with RSDs between 2.4% and 4.9% indicating that the sensor is accurate enough for detecting SSZ in biological samples.

The selectivity of the prepared detection system was evaluated by observing the changes in the fluorescence intensities of MIP-QDs bonded SSZ in the presence of various interferences. For this purpose, 20 mL of sample solutions containing 0.2 µmol L−1 of SSZ and different quantities of interferents were prepared and analyzed as described in Section 2.4. The major potentially interferences that exist in the biological samples such as some metal ions, anions, uric acid, ascorbic acid, glucose, fructose and substances with the similar chemical structures such as sulfapyridine, mesalazine), sulfadiazine, sulfamethoxazole, sulfanilamide and p-aminobenzoic acid were selected in this study. An ion or substance was considered to interfere when its existence caused a variation of more than 5% in the fluorescence intensity of SSZ. As can be seen from the results in Table 2, the common metal ions and anions had no significant effect on fluorescence responses of MIP-QDs (fluorescent quenching or enhancement) compared to SSZ. In addition, little interfering was observed with some substances toward SSZ. The obtained results confirm that the developed sensing system is highly selective toward SSZ molecules, and can provide reliable results to SSZ determination in complex matrices.

To further evaluate the efficiency of the proposed solid phase luminescence sensor, a comparison with other analytical techniques reported previously [4,16,42–44] for the analysis of SSZ in different matrices was performed. According to the results listed in Table 3, the presented method showed good linearity, low detection limit and satisfactory precision and accuracy which were comparable or better than other studies described in the literature. In addition, the developed method demonstrated the advantages of simplicity and low cost combined with a sensitive fluorescence detection. Therefore, the proposed sensor can be successfully applied to detection of SSZ in complex matrices.

In this study, a novel high selective and sensitive solid-phase luminescent sensor based on QDs modified with MIP was synthesized and applied for a selective recognition/extraction processes to SSZ determination in biological samples. The MIP-QDs-glass slide combined the high selectivity of MIPs and the good fluorescence property of QDs, providing a highly selective recognition and sensitive determination of SSZ. The developed solid phase extraction method in the presented study was based on a solventless sample preparation procedure which makes it an environmentally friendly technique to detect SSZ. In addition, the prepared MIP-QDs sensor was used to determination of SSZ in biological samples without the need to remove interferes during the analysis. The presented sensor showed long-term stability and good reproducibility with benefits of simplicity and facility of operation that makes the proposed sensor useful in the determination of SSZ in complex matrices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The Support by the Research Council of The Iran University of Science and Technology through Grant to conduct this study is gratefully acknowledged. The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Topical delivery of therapeutic agents in the treatment of inflammatory bowel disease
The pharmacokinetic profiles of oral mesalazine formulations and mesalazine pro-drugs used in the management of ulcerative colitis
Electroanalytical determination of sulfasalazine in pharmaceutical and biological samples using molecularly imprinted polymer modified carbon paste electrode
Favourable issue of a fulminant hepatitis associated with sulfasalazine DRESS syndrome without liver transplantation
Determination of sulfonamide antibiotics in wastewater: a comparison of solid phase microextraction and solid phase extraction methods
Simple and rapid method for the simultaneous determination of the eight main metabolites and conjugates of sulphasalazine in human plasma, urine and faeces using dynamically modified silica
J. Chromatogr. B Biomed. Sci. Appl.
A simple and rapid liquid chromatographic method for the determination of major metabolites of sulfasalazine in biological fluids

Determination of sulphasalazine and its main metabolite sulphapyridine and 5-aminosalicylic acid in human plasma by liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry and its application to a pharmacokinetic study
Simultaneous determination of sulfasalazine and its metabolites sulfapyridine and N-acetylsulfapyridine in human serum by ion-pair high-performance liquid chromatography using a polymer-based column
Quantitative HPLC analysis of mebeverine, mesalazine, sulphasalazine and dispersible aspirin stored in a Venalink monitored dosage system with co-prescribed medicines
Quantitative determination of sulfasalazine by near-infrared spectroscopy and multivariate analysis in reflectance mode with a fibre-optic probe
In vitro antioxidant properties of potential biotransformation products of salicylate, sulphasalazine and amidopyrine
Immunochemical method for sulfasalazine determination in human plasma
Pressurized liquid extraction combined with capillary electrophoresis–mass spectrometry as an improved methodology for the determination of sulfonamide residues in meat
Determination of sulfasalazine in sulfasalazine tablets using silver nanoparticles
Probing the cytotoxicity of semiconductor quantum dots
Quantum dot-based assay for Cu2+ quantification in bacterial cell culture
Luminescent CdS quantum dots as selective ion probes
Development and application of molecularly imprinted polymer – Mn-doped ZnS quantum dot fluorescent optosensing for cocaine screening in oral fluid and serum
A new determining method of copper(II) ions at ng mL-1 levels based on quenching of the water-soluble nanocrystals fluorescence
Mercaptoacetic acid capped CdS quantum dots as fluorescence single shot probe for mercury(II)
Electrochemical quantum dots-based magneto-immunoassay for detection of HE4 protein on metal film-modified screen-printed carbon electrodes
Triethanolamine-capped CdSe quantum dots as fluorescent sensors for reciprocal recognition of mercury (II) and iodide in aqueous solution
Molecularly imprinted polymers for the determination of organophosphorus pesticides in complex samples

A novel molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor based on graphene quantum dots coated on hollow nickel nanospheres with high sensitivity and selectivity for the rapid determination of bisphenol S
A hybrid molecularly imprinted polymer coated quantum dot nanocomposite optosensor for highly sensitive and selective determination of salbutamol in animal feeds and meat samples
A molecularly-imprinted-electrochemical-sensor modified with nano-carbon-dots with high sensitivity and selectivity for rapid determination of glucose
Synthesis of novel monomeric graphene quantum dots and corresponding nanocomposite with molecularly imprinted polymer for electrochemical detection of an anticancerous ifosfamide drug
